coredata.dc:title: A new disposable microfluidic electrochemical paper-based device for the simultaneous determination of clinical biomarkers
coredata.dc:creator: Cincotto, Fernando H.
Fava, Elson L.
Moraes, Fernando C.
Fatibello-Filho, Orlando
Faria, Ronaldo C.
coredata.dc:description: A new disposable microfluidic electrochemical paper-based device (ePAD) consisting of two spot sensors in the same working electrode for the simultaneous determination of uric acid and creatinine was developed. The spot 1 surface was modified with graphene quantum dots for direct uric acid oxidation and spot 2 surface modified with graphene quantum dots, creatininase and a ruthenium electrochemical mediator for creatinine oxidation. The ePAD was employed to construct an electrochemical sensor (based on square wave voltammetry analysis) for the simultaneous determination of uric acid and creatinine in the 0.010–3.0 µmol L−1 range. The device showed excellent analytical performance with a very low simultaneous detection limit of 8.4 nmol L−1 to uric acid and 3.7 nmol L−1 to creatinine and high selectivity. The ePAD was applied to the rapid and successful determination of those clinical biomarkers in human urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Disposable electrochemical device
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Creatininase
Simultaneous detection
Clinical samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modifications of working electrodes with GQDs for UA determination and with GQDs and creatininase for creatinine determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Steps involving the construction of ePAD: (a) polyester sheet with the screen-printed working electrode (WE1 and WE2) and a hole cut for sample injection, (b) polyester sheet with reference (RE)and counter electrodes (CE1 and CE2), (c) filter paper microfluidic pathway, and (d) scheme and image of the ePAD ready.
Representative TEM (A, B) and HRTEM (C) images of GQDs nanocrystalline particles. Bottle containing GQDs dispersion under excitation of ultraviolet light irradiation (D).
SWV recorded at spot 1, the carbon-based electrode and electrode modified with GQDs toward 1.0 µmol L−1 uric acid in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.0.

Cyclic voltammograms at the GQDs/hexaammine-ruthenium(III) chloride electrode before (black and red line) and after (blue line) addition of the creatinine enzyme. Study recorded in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.0 (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

SWV recorded at spot 2, comparison of different composition of electrode materials toward 1.0 µmol L−1 creatinine in sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7 (a) blank (sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7), (b) GQDs, (c) GQDs/enzyme, (d) bare electrode/mediator, (e) GQDs/mediator, (f) bare electrode/mediator/enzyme and (g) GQDs/mediator/enzyme.
SWV recorded at the spot 1 of the ePAD over the uric acid 0.01 µmol L−1 to 3.0 µmol L−1 concentration range in sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.
SWV recorded at the spot 2 of the new disposable electrochemical device over the creatinine 10.0 nmol L−1 to 3.0 µmol L−1 concentration range in sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.

(A) SW voltammograms recorded at the new disposable electrochemical device for 0 (a), 0.01 µmol L−1 (b), 0.05 µmol L−1 (c), 0.12 µmol L−1 (d), 0.20 µmol L−1 (e), 0.40 µmol L−1 (f), 0.60 µmol L−1 (g), 0.85 µmol L−1 (h), 1.3 µmol L−1 (i), 2.0 µmol L−1 (j) and 3.0 µmol L−1 (l) creatinine and uric acid mix solution. (B) Analytical curve constructed for creatinine and uric acid at the ePAD.

Schematic representation of the working electrodes. Spot 1 was modified with GQDs for directly UA determination and Spot 2 was modified with GQDs/creatininase/hexaammine-ruthenium(III) chloride for creatinine determination.
Reactions that describe the proposed electrocatalytic mechanism.
Comparison of the analytical performance of relevant electrochemical sensors for creatinine and uric acid.

head.ce:title: A new disposable microfluidic electrochemical paper-based device for the simultaneous determination of clinical biomarkers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Federal University of São Carlos, 13565-905 São Carlos, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry Department, Federal University of São Carlos

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new disposable microfluidic electrochemical paper-based device (ePAD) consisting of two spot sensors in the same working electrode for the simultaneous determination of uric acid and creatinine was developed. The spot 1 surface was modified with graphene quantum dots for direct uric acid oxidation and spot 2 surface modified with graphene quantum dots, creatininase and a ruthenium electrochemical mediator for creatinine oxidation. The ePAD was employed to construct an electrochemical sensor (based on square wave voltammetry analysis) for the simultaneous determination of uric acid and creatinine in the 0.010–3.0 µmol L−1 range. The device showed excellent analytical performance with a very low simultaneous detection limit of 8.4 nmol L−1 to uric acid and 3.7 nmol L−1 to creatinine and high selectivity. The ePAD was applied to the rapid and successful determination of those clinical biomarkers in human urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Intense efforts have been directed in recent years to the development of low-cost and simple microfluidic platforms for analytical applications. Microfluidic devices (μFDs) are very attractive for important areas, such as in clinical and food analysis, with significant advantages over conventional wet bench methods by reducing the use of reagents and samples and decreasing the analysis time [1–3]. With μFDs, it is now possible to increase parallel processing ability, as well as reliability and sensitivity, through automation, integration and miniaturisation [4,5]. In addition, in recent decades, large advances in technology have yielded significant improvements in the manufacture of microfluidic devices that impacted positively in the development of new point of care systems [6].

The μFDs are generally constructed with polymers or glasses using a variety of techniques to produce the microfluidic channels. Several techniques have focused on the development of μFDs employed using materials, such as silicon, glass, paper, low temperature co-fired ceramics and polymers. Among these materials, silicon has been widely used as a result of its well-known properties and well-established manufacturing methods based on the photolithography of the semiconductor industry [7–9]. With the development of soft lithography, poly(dimethylsiloxane) has gained increasing popularity and has played a key role in promoting significant improvements in research on microfluidic devices due to the relatively inexpensive and simple manufacturing procedures involved [10,11]. Recently, we developed a simple and low-cost rapid prototyping method based on the use of an inexpensive home cutter printer for the construction of disposable microfluidic electrochemical systems [12,13]. The device features an array of carbon-based electrodes screen-printed over polyester sheets sandwiched using a double adhesive card with the microfluidic channel interfaced to a syringe pump and a sample injector for high sensitive immunosensing applications.

Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) have been widely applied in the detection of clinically relevant compounds as a result of their intrinsic advantages, including low reagent consumption, low cost and the possibility that they unfold by integrating an entire laboratory into a single chip with no need for pumping systems [14]. The μPADs have been constructed using soft lithography and, most widely, a wax printer as a way to create a microfluidic channel by generating hydrophilic barriers in the filter paper [15–17]. Despite all the advantages, soft lithography requires special conditions, including a clean room with light control and different photomasks for each new microfluidic pattern. In addition, the wax printer no longer being sold by the main manufacturer brings new challenges in implementing this method. An alternative for the fast and easy development of microfluidic channels involves cutting the paper by using a CO2 laser and cutting presses [18,19]. However, these methods present limitations, such as the need for personal training and the high cost of maintenance. Recently, we developed 2D and 3D μPADs based on use of an inexpensive home cutter printer for easily cut microfluidic patterns on filter paper [20]. The μPADs were applied in accurate colorimetric detection of analytes of clinical interest.

The integration of μPADs with electrochemical detection brings new functions and capabilities to microfluidic systems, allowing for the detection of different compounds with higher accuracy in a simple way. There are different strategies to assemble the electrodes in the paper microfluidic channel that include the use of external electrodes from commercial disposable electrochemical cell or the use of solid electrodes as pencil graphite or metal wire and the integration of the electrodes directly on the paper by inkjet printing, screen printing or directly drawing with a pencil. One of the great attractions for the use of screen printing electrodes is their relatively low cost due to the possibility of large-scale manufacturing, in addition to the possibility of constructing this type of device in different conformations [21].

In this work, we demonstrated the construction of a new electrochemical paper-based microfluidic device (ePAD) that was successfully applied for the simultaneous detection of uric acid (UA) and creatinine (CNN) in human urine samples. The ePAD was entirely constructed using an inexpensive home cutter printer from cutting the filter paper to create the microfluidic channel through the electrode by creating the screen-printed electrodes over polyester film.

All chemicals employed in this work were of analytical grade. CNN, UA, ascorbic acid, glutaraldehyde, sodium phosphate, sodium hydrogen phosphate creatininase from microorganisms (CA, EC 3.5.2.10, 231 U/mg) and hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). A 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution was used as a supporting electrolyte. All solutions were prepared with water purified in a Millipore Milli-Q system (resistivity > 18 MΩ cm). Citric acid and sodium hydroxide purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany) were used in the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a PGSTAT 302 Autolab electrochemical system (Eco Chemie, Netherlands) monitored with NOVA (Eco Chemie, Netherlands) software. All measurements were carried out at room temperature. To cut the paper, adhesives and polyester sheet, a craft printer Silhouette CAMEO (Silhouette Brazil) was used and all the patterns were drawn using Silhouette Studio version 2.7.4 software.

The details of the ePAD construction are shown in Fig. 1. The ePAD developed is composed of a filter paper cut in the desired pattern using the home cutter printer and two sheets of polyester, one with the hole for sample injection and the screen-printed working electrodes and the other sheet with the screen-printed counter and reference electrodes. The ePAD electrodes were deposited over the polyester sheet (transparency film for laser printers) by a simple screen-printed method previously reported [13]. Briefly, the method consisted of using the cutter printer to cut in a vinyl adhesive the pattern of the electrodes and used as a negative mask to form the stencil for screen-printing. The cut vinyl adhesive was attached to the polyester sheet and following carbon ink (Electrodag 423SS, Henkel Ltd, USA) was applied using a rubber squeegee and cured for 30 min at 90 °C. The procedure was applied for all electrodes. For the reference electrode, after applying the carbon ink, it was kept dry for 15 min at room temperature and Ag/AgCl ink (C2051014P10, Gwent Electronic Materials Ltd., UK) was applied using a small brush and cured for 30 min at 60 °C. After this, the vinyl adhesive was removed, and the sheets with the electrodes were ready to use in the ePAD. The microfluidic channel was constructed using filter paper, as previously reported [20]. In a similar way, the pattern of the channel was drawn and cut using the cutter printer. To avoid tears and ruptures in the soft filter paper, a low tack adhesive vinyl was used to cover the paper that could be easily detached without removing cellulose fibres and leaving no residue. Second, the paper was cut on the cutter printer using a perforation cutting procedure, followed by overlapped continuous cutting. This method allowed for cutting the paper into different patterns with good precision. After manufacturing the electrodes and the paper microfluidic channel, the ePAD was constructed by sandwiching the sheets and the paper using double tape cut with the cutter printer, as represented in Fig. 1. After that, the device was sealed using a pressing machine, with the heating system off, for better adhesion. A photograph of the actual device after it was finalised is shown in Fig. 1c.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were performed with a JEOL transmission electron microscope, operating at 200 kV. The as prepared GQDs were deposited onto ultrathin carbon-coated copper grids.

GQDs were synthesised by pyrolysing citric acid and dispersing the carbonised products into alkaline solutions [22]. Briefly, 1.0 g of citric acid was inserted into a beaker and heated to 200 °C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange solution. Then, to prepare the GQDs, 50.0 mL of a 10.0 mg mL−1 NaOH solution was added into the orange homogenous solution dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modifications of working electrodes with GQDs for UA determination and with GQDs and creatininase for creatinine determination
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the working electrode, the two spots of working electrodes of the ePAD were first rinsed with distilled water. Next, the two spots were coated with GQDs by depositing 10 µL of an aqueous dispersion of the GQDs on the surface and kept until dry at room temperature. The spot of UA determination was then ready to use. To prepare the enzymatic electrode, in the second spot, the enzyme creatininase was further immobilised on the nanostructured electrode surface by dropping 10 µL of a 1.5 mg mL−1 enzyme solution in a 100 mmol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7, and mixing with 10 µL of a 2% (v/v) glutaraldehyde solution. The electrode was kept at 4 °C for 1 h and then washed several times with cold 100 mmol L−1 of sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7. After this, the electrochemical mediator was added to the enzymatic electrode surface by dropping 10 µL of a 0.1 mmol L−1 of hexaammineruthenium (III) chloride solution in 100 mmol L−1 of sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7 and kept at 4 °C for 30 min. The electrode was then washed several times with cold 100 mmol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7. Finally, the ePAD was assembled, sealed and stored at 4 °C before use.

Scheme 1 illustrates the structures used to prepare the two working electrodes, one spot for direct UA determination and the second spot was assembled with the creatininase biosensor for creatinine determination. To modify the electrodes, first, the GQD material was prepared through a one-pot scheme by melting citric acid and dispersing the carbonised products into alkaline solutions. Through this process, the nanocrystalline particles of GQDs were formed rapidly and, simultaneously, the alkaline solution stabilised the nanoparticles with the hydroxyl groups, thus ensuring the non-agglomeration of the GQDs. After that, the simple immobilisation of the GQDs was performed on the surface of the working electrode containing the two spots.
Fig. 2 shows representative TEM and HRTEM images of the GQD nanocrystalline particles. Highly poly-dispersed sphere-like nanoparticles with average diameters of

The electrochemical sensors presented in the ePAD were first evaluated separately to investigate the behaviour of spots 1 and 2. The GQD coated spot 1 was characterised by square wave voltammetry (SWV) in 100 mmol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7, to evaluate the influence of the monocrystalline particles on the electrochemical behaviour ( Fig. 3) in the absence (black) and presence of UA. Compared to the carbon-based electrode (bare electrode), the background current in the presence of GQDs increased greatly due to the larger electroactive area of the nanocomposite film on the electrode surface. In the presence of UA, the bare electrode presents an oxidation process with a shoulder at + 180 mV, as can be observed in Fig. 3. Alternatively, one well-defined oxidation peak at + 61 mV was observed in response to the GQD modification in UA. In addition, comparing the oxidation potential of the bare electrode and GQD-modified spot 1, in relation to the UA, it was possible to observe a shift of 120 mV to less positive potentials, indicating the electrocatalytic properties of the GQDs.

As illustrated in Scheme 1, the ePAD was assembled by coating the two spots with the GQD nanocrystalline particles and further immobilisation of the enzyme creatininase in spot 2 by using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking reagent. It is well known that glutaraldehyde can form stable cross-linking adducts by reaction with many nucleophiles, such as amine, thiol, hydroxyl and imidazole groups [24], and these properties have been largely employed to prepare immobilised biocatalysts, polymeric films, microparticles, hydrogels and membranes [22,25,26]. The spot 2 enzyme electrode was further employed to determine creatinine by SWV using hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride as an electrochemical mediator, the hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride is present only in the modification of this spot for creatinine determination.

It was important to ensure that the analytical signal recorded with the spot 2 enzyme electrode was related to the creatininase-mediated transformation of creatinine in the presence of hexaammine-ruthenium(III) chloride. In this sense, a control experiment was performed by recording cyclic voltammograms at the GQD electrode, in the presence of creatinine, before and after creatininase enzyme addition in the electrolyte solution. As can be analysed in Fig. 4, in the absence of the creatininase enzyme, adding creatinine in solution, no redox process can be observed. However, well-defined redox peaks, with the peak height increasing in the creatininase enzyme presence,were observed after addition of the enzyme, supporting the proposed mechanism below.

The reactions that describe the proposed electrocatalytic mechanism involved in the enzyme-mediated transformation of creatinine at the electrode surface using hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride as an electrochemical mediator are shown in Scheme 2. Through this mechanism, creatinine can be converted to creatine by the enzyme creatininase (reaction 1). The presence of the electrochemical mediator (hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride), ruthenium III reacts with creatine, and is converted to ruthenium II in a chemical reaction (reaction 2). Concomitantly with reaction 2, ruthenium II is oxidised to ruthenium III in an electrochemical reaction (reaction 3).

To ensure that the recorded analytical signal confirms the proposed electrochemical mechanism, a study is related to the transformation of creatinine in the presence of hexaammine-ruthenium(III) chloride and the absence of enzyme, a control experiment was performed by recording cyclic voltammograms at the GQD electrode (Fig. 1S (Supporting information)). The experiment was carried out in 100 mmol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7, containing a fixed concentration of 1.0 mmol L−1 of hexaammine-ruthenium(III) chloride, and successive additions of creatine concentrations; note that creatine is the product of the creatinine reaction. As can be seen in this study, well-defined redox peaks were found, with peak height increasing with creatine concentration. These cyclic voltammetry experiments were performed at low scan rates (15 mV s−1) to more clearly see the effect of creatine concentration on the resulting peaks.

As control experiments, voltammograms of spot 2 modified with different combinations of the biosensing interface components were also recorded. As can be observed in Fig. 5, the electrodes showed voltammetry responses, which can be ascribed to the electrochemical reaction of hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride on the electrode surface. The presence of GQDs on the sensing interface caused a catalytic effect on the analytical determination of creatinine by large electrochemical signals, as obtained by combining active creatininase, GQDs and the electrochemical mediator on the sensing surface (Fig. 5). This demonstrates that the enzyme is needed for an efficient catalytic transformation of creatinine, and accordingly, the proposed interface architecture is a good choice for the development of an electrochemical biosensor for creatinine.

Taking into account the larger electroanalytical responses obtained by SWV, the new disposable electrochemical device measurements were performed by using this electrochemical method. As is reported in Fig. 2S (Supporting information), larger analytical responses were achieved by coating the spot 2 of the device with an immobilisation of 1.5 mg mL−1 creatininase using 2% (v/v) glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking agent.

The experimental conditions for the new disposable electrochemical device were optimised by evaluating the influence of different parameters on the SWV response toward a 1.0 µmol L−1 creatinine and UA mixed solution in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer at pH 7.

The optimisation studies for the SWV to the ePAD of UA (spot 1) and creatinine (spot 2) determination were evaluated. It was also demonstrated that larger peak currents were measured with a 0.1 mmol L−1 hexaammine-ruthenium (III) chloride concentration, as can be observed in Fig. 3S-A (Supporting information), as well as for a SWV modulation amplitude of 20 mV, a frequency of 45 Hz and a step potential of 0.5 mV (Fig. 3SB–D).

The effect of pH on the analytical signal of the new disposable electrochemical device toward two spots of the sensor was measured by SWV. Interestingly, higher analytical signals were recorded by using 0.1 mol L−1 sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7, as can be observed in Fig. 4S (Supporting information). Accordingly, these working conditions were selected for further measurements.

Before evaluating the new disposable electrochemical device fully, each spot of the device was studied separately. Fig. 6 shows typical SWV recorded at spot 1 of the ePAD upon successive additions of UA. The current peak values increased with the UA concentration exhibiting a linear behaviour over the 0.01 µmol L−1–3.0 µmol L−1 concentration range.

Fig. 7 shows SWV recorded at spot 2 of the ePAD upon successive additions of creatinine. The current peak values increased with the creatinine concentration exhibiting a linear behaviour over the 0.01 µmol L−1–3.0 µmol L−1 concentration range.

After the two spots of the new disposable electrochemical device were studied in the determinations of UA and creatinine separately, the device was completely evaluated. Fig. 8 shows square wave voltammograms recorded at the new disposable electrochemical device upon successive additions of the creatinine and UA mixed solution.

L

]

This range of linear response was narrower than others previously reported for electrochemical device for creatinine and UA ( Table 1). However, it ensures the accurate determination of the substances at the nanomolar concentration level.

Very low detection limits of 3.7 and 8.4 nmol L−1 for creatinine and UA, respectively, were estimated for this new disposable electrochemical device. This value was calculated according to the 3Sb/m criterion, where m is the slope of the analytical curve and Sb was estimated as the standard deviation of 10 different SWV signals recorded for the lowest substances concentration measured in the analytical graph (10.0 nmol L−1). As can be observed in Table 1, this low limit of detection ranks among the best obtained for the electrochemical detection of the substances. However, our new disposable electrochemical device is characterised by a simple design, low cost and easy operation, in contrast with those affinity-based biosensors previously described. These excellent analytical properties could be associated to the use of GQDs as nanostructured scaffolds for the covalent immobilisation of the enzyme.

The selectivity of the new disposable electrochemical device was evaluated by measuring the SWV responses generated by four different potential interfering substances at a 10.0 µmol L−1 concentration and comparing with that obtained for a 1.0 µmol L−1 creatinine and UA mixed solution under similar measuring conditions. Low analytical signals were recorded for ascorbic acid (0.8%), hydroquinone (0.9%), dopamine (1.3%) and catechol (1.1%), suggesting a high selectivity of the new disposable electrochemical device for creatinine and UA.

The long-term stability of the new disposable electrochemical device was estimated by storing the device at 4 °C under dry conditions. The devices were periodically tested by measuring the 1.0 µmol L−1 creatinine and UA mixed solution, the analytical response was compared with those obtained the first day of use (Fig. 4S in Supporting information). The ePAD retained more than 95% of the initial electroanalytical activity after one week of storage, but longer times at 4 °C produced a progressive loss of analytical response reaching about 84% and 55% of the initial activity after two and four weeks of storage, respectively.

Human urine is a major source of creatinine and UA compounds. The determination of the substances in urinary samples is relevant to evaluate the action of kidneys, and accordingly, to detect potential malfunction of kidneys that are commonly associated with many diseases (discussed in the introduction session). For these reasons, the new disposable electrochemical device was employed to determine the creatinine and UA content in spiked real urine samples. To achieve this, urine samples were spiked with a creatinine and UA mixed solution, properly diluted and measured with the new disposable electrochemical device (Fig. 5S in Supporting information). High recovery values of 101.5 ± 0.7% and 98.9 ± 0.5% were obtained for real urine samples, demonstrating the analytical reliability of the new proposed disposable electrochemical device to simultaneously quantify creatinine and UA in real samples.

The device was developed with one working electrode divided into two spots, so that each of the spots was responsible for the detection of one analyte. The spots architecture involved the modification of the spot 1 surface with graphene quantum dots for directly uric acid oxidation and spot 2 modification with graphene quantum dots, creatininase enzyme and a ruthenium electrochemical mediator to creatinine oxidation. Thus, this new disposable electrochemical device allowed determining simultaneously these two biological biomarkers by square-wave voltammetry with high sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility. This proposed method was successfully used to determine uric acid and creatinine in real human urine samples with high recovery values.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Brazilian agencies FAPESP (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo) for funding resources to Cincotto, F.H. (2016/16565-5), Fava, E.L. (2016/10012-4), Moraes, F.C. (2016/12926-3 and 2017/10118-0), CAPES (Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil) and CNPq Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico) ((444150/2014-5, 400412/2016-0) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.11.022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physics and applications of microfluidics in biology

Faradaic ion concentration polarization on a paper fluidic platform

Microfluidic device for real-time formulation of reagents and their subsequent encapsulation into double emulsions
Rapid prototyping of microfluidic systems in poly(dimethylsiloxane)
Simple and rapid fabrication of disposable carbon-based electrochemical cells using an electronic craft cutter for sensor and biosensor applications
Fully disposable microfluidic electrochemical device for detection of estrogen receptor alpha breast cancer biomarker
Sliding-strip microfluidic device enables ELISA on paper
Metabolic response of lung cancer cells to radiation in a paper-based 3D cell culture system
Sliding-strip microfluidic device enables ELISA on paper
A paper-based pop-up electrochemical device for analysis of beta-hydroxybutyrate
A simple method to produce 2D and 3D microfluidic paper-based analytical devices for clinical analysis
New directions in screen printed electroanalytical sensors: an overview of recent developments
Water proof and strength retention properties of thermoplastic starch based biocomposites modified with glutaraldehyde
High performance electrochemical sensors for dopamine and epinephrine using nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots obtained under controlled chronoamperometric conditions
Glutaraldehyde: behavior in aqueous solution, reaction with proteins, and application to enzyme crosslinking
Versatility of divinylsulfone supports permits the tuning of CALB properties during its immobilization
Improved method for synthesis of cysteine modified hyaluronic acid for in situ hydrogel formation

Electrochemical biosensor for creatinine based on the immobilization of creatininase, creatinase and sarcosine oxidase onto a ferrocene/horseradish peroxidase/gold nanoparticles/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/Teflon composite electrode

Multienzyme‐immobilized modified polypropylene membrane for an amperometric creatinine biosensor
Immobilization of creatininase, creatinase and sarcosine oxidase on iron oxide nanoparticles/chitosan-g-polyaniline modified Pt electrode for detection of creatinine

The Enhanced Photo-Electrochemical Detection of Uric Acid on Au Nanoparticles Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode

Microfabricated disposable nanosensor based on CdSe quantum dot/ionic liquid-mediated hollow fiber-pencil graphite electrode for simultaneous electrochemical quantification of uric acid and creatinine in human samples
